parts.context: A conversation between a travel agent and a customer.
parts.audio_script: Agent: Good morning, Globe Travel Agency. How can I help you today? Customer: Hi, I’d like to book a holiday for next week. Agent: Certainly. May I know your preferred destination? Customer: I’m thinking about Paris. Agent: Wonderful choice. When would you like to travel? Customer: I’d prefer to leave on Tuesday. Agent: And how many nights will you be staying? Customer: Four nights, please. Agent: Will you be travelling alone or with someone? Customer: With my sister. Agent: Great. May I have your full name, please? Customer: Yes, it’s Emily Carter. Agent: Thank you, Ms. Carter. Do you have any dietary requirements? Customer: Yes, I’m vegetarian. Agent: Noted. Now, let me tell you about our packages. The basic package includes breakfast, but not dinner or flights. Customer: I see. What about sightseeing tours? Agent: Those are included in the premium package. Customer: I think I’ll go with the basic one for now. Agent: No problem. I’ll just need your contact number to confirm the booking. Customer: It’s 07923 654 112. Agent: Thank you. We’ll send you the confirmation shortly.

question_sets.instruction: Complete the notes below. Write ONE WORD ONLY AND/OR A NUMBER for each answer.

questions.question_text: Customer wants to travel on ______.
Destination: ______.
Number of nights: ______.
Travelling with: ______.
Customer’s surname: ______.
Dietary requirement: ______.

question_sets.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B, or C.

questions.question_text: What is included in the basic package?
Who is the customer travelling with?
What is required to confirm the booking?
Which package includes sightseeing tours?

parts.context: A guide giving information about a city park.
parts.audio_script: Guide: Welcome to Greenfield City Park. The park opens at 6 a.m. and closes at 9 p.m. daily. The main entrance is on Oak Street, opposite the library. If you walk straight ahead, you’ll find the children’s playground on your left and the rose garden on your right. The café is next to the playground, and the public toilets are behind the café. For those interested in sports, the tennis courts are located in the northeast corner, while the football field is in the southwest. There are picnic areas throughout the park, but the largest one is near the lake, which is at the far end of the park. Please remember that dogs must be kept on a lead at all times, and cycling is only allowed on designated paths. Enjoy your visit!

question_sets.instruction: Label the map below. Choose FIVE answers from the box and write the correct letter, A–H, next to questions 11–15.

Rose garden

parts.context: A discussion between two students and their tutor about a group project.
parts.audio_script: Tutor: So, how is your group project on renewable energy coming along? Student 1: We’ve divided the work. I’m researching solar power, and Alex is focusing on wind energy. Student 2: Yes, and we’re planning to meet twice a week to discuss our findings. Tutor: That’s good. Have you decided how to present your results? Student 1: We’re thinking of making a poster and giving a short talk. Tutor: Excellent. Remember to include data and real-life examples. Student 2: We’ll do that. We’re also planning to interview a local expert. Tutor: That’s a great idea. When is your deadline? Student 1: It’s the 15th of next month. Tutor: Make sure you finish your research by the end of this month, so you have time to prepare the presentation. Student 2: We will. Tutor: Good luck!

question_sets.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B, or C.

questions.question_text: What is the topic of the group project?
How often do the students plan to meet?
What will the students include in their presentation?
Who will the students interview?
When is the project deadline?

question_sets.instruction: Match each student with the topic they are researching.

parts.context: A university lecture on the topic of sleep and memory.
parts.audio_script: Lecturer: Good afternoon. Today, we’ll discuss the relationship between sleep and memory. Research shows that sleep plays a vital role in consolidating memories. There are two main types of sleep: REM, or rapid eye movement sleep, and non-REM sleep. Both are important, but REM sleep is particularly linked to learning and creativity. Studies have found that students who get enough sleep after studying perform better in tests. Lack of sleep can impair concentration and make it harder to remember information. To improve memory, it’s recommended to maintain a regular sleep schedule, avoid caffeine late in the day, and create a restful environment. In addition, short naps can help boost memory, especially after learning new material. However, excessive napping may disrupt nighttime sleep. In summary, good sleep habits are essential for effective learning and memory retention.

question_sets.instruction: Complete the notes below. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS for each answer.

questions.question_text: Sleep helps to ______ memories.

Excessive napping may disrupt ______ sleep.

question_sets.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B, or C.

questions.question_text: What is the main topic of the lecture?

What is recommended to improve memory?
What can short naps help with?

passages.title: The History of the Modern Bicycle
passages.content: The modern bicycle is the result of nearly two centuries of innovation. The earliest known bicycle, called the 'Draisine' or 'running machine', was invented by Baron Karl von Drais in 1817. This wooden vehicle had two wheels and was propelled by the rider pushing their feet against the ground. It had no pedals, chain, or brakes, and was made almost entirely of wood. In the 1860s, French inventors Pierre Michaux and his son Ernest added pedals to the front wheel, creating the 'velocipede', also known as the 'boneshaker' due to its uncomfortable ride on cobblestone streets. The next major development came in the 1870s with the 'penny-farthing', which featured a large front wheel and a much smaller rear wheel. This design allowed for greater speed but was difficult to mount and dangerous to ride. The breakthrough that led to the modern bicycle came in the 1880s, when John Kemp Starley developed the 'safety bicycle'. This model had two wheels of equal size, a chain drive to the rear wheel, and pneumatic tyres, making it safer and more comfortable. The safety bicycle quickly became popular and set the standard for future designs. Over the 20th century, bicycles continued to evolve. Lightweight steel frames replaced heavy iron ones, gears were introduced to make riding easier on hills, and new materials like aluminium and carbon fibre made bikes even lighter and stronger. Today, bicycles are used for transportation, sport, and recreation all over the world. They are valued for their efficiency, environmental friendliness, and health benefits. In many cities, bike-sharing schemes and dedicated cycling lanes have encouraged more people to cycle. The bicycle remains a symbol of innovation and sustainable transport.

question_sets.instruction: Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 1? Write TRUE, FALSE, or NOT GIVEN.

questions.question_text: The first bicycle was made entirely of wood.
The 'velocipede' was comfortable to ride on all surfaces.

The safety bicycle had two wheels of equal size.

Modern bicycles are made from a variety of materials.

passages.title: The Benefits and Challenges of Urban Green Spaces
passages.content: Urban green spaces, such as parks, gardens, and nature reserves, play a crucial role in improving the quality of life in cities. They provide residents with opportunities for recreation, relaxation, and social interaction. Studies have shown that access to green spaces can reduce stress, improve mental health, and encourage physical activity. In addition, these areas help to reduce air pollution, lower urban temperatures, and support biodiversity by providing habitats for various species. However, creating and maintaining green spaces in densely populated cities is not without challenges. Land is often expensive and limited, making it difficult to allocate space for parks. Furthermore, green spaces require ongoing maintenance, including watering, landscaping, and waste management. There is also the issue of safety, as poorly maintained parks can attract crime. Despite these challenges, many city planners are finding innovative solutions. Some cities are converting unused land, such as old railway lines, into parks. Others are encouraging rooftop gardens and vertical greenery on buildings. Community involvement is also key, with local residents participating in the design and upkeep of green spaces. In conclusion, while urban green spaces present certain difficulties, their benefits to health, environment, and community well-being make them an essential part of modern cities.

passages.title: The Rise of Online Education
passages.content: In recent years, online education has transformed the way people learn. With the development of high-speed internet and digital platforms, students can now access courses from universities around the world without leaving their homes. Online education offers flexibility, allowing learners to study at their own pace and fit their studies around work or family commitments. It also provides opportunities for people in remote areas to gain qualifications that were previously out of reach. However, online learning is not without its drawbacks. Some students find it difficult to stay motivated without face-to-face interaction. Technical issues, such as poor internet connections, can also disrupt learning. In addition, not all courses are suitable for online delivery, especially those requiring practical skills or laboratory work. Despite these challenges, the popularity of online education continues to grow. Many universities now offer 'blended' courses, combining online and in-person teaching. Employers are increasingly recognising online qualifications, although some still prefer traditional degrees. The future of education is likely to involve a mix of online and classroom learning, as technology continues to evolve and new teaching methods are developed.

tasks.instruction: You should spend about 20 minutes on this task. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.
tasks.data_description: The table below shows the percentage of households in five different countries (USA, UK, Japan, Brazil, and India) that owned a computer in the years 2000, 2005, and 2010. In 2000, the USA had the highest percentage at 51%, followed by the UK at 38%, Japan at 30%, Brazil at 12%, and India at 5%. By 2005, ownership had increased in all countries: USA (65%), UK (54%), Japan (48%), Brazil (25%), and India (13%). In 2010, the figures rose further: USA (78%), UK (70%), Japan (66%), Brazil (42%), and India (27%). The data shows a steady increase in computer ownership in all countries, with the USA consistently leading and India having the lowest percentage throughout the period.

tasks.instruction: You should spend about 40 minutes on this task. Write about the following topic. Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples. Write at least 250 words.
tasks.prompt: Some people believe that the best way to improve public health is by increasing the number of sports facilities. Others, however, think that this would have little effect on public health and that other measures are required. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.
